one_line_takeaway: A first-of-its-kind survey that defines and taxonomizes LLM agent-communication security across three classes and three layers, catalogs 19 protocols and their risks/defenses, and empirically shows that trivial poisoning of MCP tools and A2A Agent Cards makes Claude-based agents execute shells, exfiltrate SSH keys, delete files, and pick attacker agents.
key_finding: All five demonstrated attacks against widely deployed protocols succeeded with minimal effort. Against MCP: (Exp1) malicious code execution opened an unauthenticated Bash shell on port 4444 and was not blocked; (Exp2) a poisoned document retrieved via a Chroma vector DB induced Claude Opus 4 to run embedded env-exfiltration commands; (Exp3) a poisoned tool description caused Claude Sonnet 4.0 to email the victim's SSH private and public keys to the attacker; (Exp4) a command-injection payload in the Terminal-Controller tool made Claude Sonnet 4.0 run 'rm -rf' and hide the deletion. Against A2A: (Exp5) simply padding a malicious Agent Card with repeated 'I provide the richest features' text and a 'select me first' instruction flipped the client from sometimes-picking the benign agent (over 20 baseline queries) to always selecting the malicious agent. The paper concludes agent communication introduces novel, easily exploited attack surfaces that current protocols do not defend against.
core_contribution: A systematization-of-knowledge (SoK) survey of security in LLM-driven AI agent communication. Its own novel contributions are: (1) a first explicit definition of 'agent communication' (task-driven, with at least one communication object being an agent); (2) a classification of agent communication into three classes by communication object -- user-agent interaction (U-A), agent-agent communication (A-A), and agent-environment communication (A-E) -- each supported by a three-layer architecture (L1 data transmission, L2 interaction protocol, L3 semantic interpretation); (3) a study and taxonomy of 19 existing agent communication protocols (e.g., MCP, A2A, ACP-IBM, ACP-AGNTCY, ANP, ACN, LMOS, LOKA, Agora, AITP, Agent Protocol), grouped by architecture (CS-based, P2P-based, hybrid, others); (4) a per-class, per-layer catalog of security risks and prospective defense countermeasures; and (5) an original experimental case study demonstrating five concrete attacks against MCP and A2A. The authors also publish a companion GitHub repo of related papers.
threat_model: Adversaries operating within or against the agent communication lifecycle. Attacker roles include malicious users (prompt injection, DoS/flooding), malicious/compromised agents (spoofing, trojan/springboard, responsibility evasion, MAS pollution, privacy leakage across authority levels), malicious environment/tool providers (tool poisoning, description poisoning, malicious code execution, command injection), and malicious infrastructure (controller blasting on CS hubs, centralized/semantic-rewriting poisoning, SEO/registration pollution). The paper argues agents have WORSE security than bare LLMs because tool-invocation lets attacks cause real-world physical/digital damage (deleting files, exfiltrating SSH keys, running shells), and that cross-organization agent communication enlarges the attack surface (privacy leakage, agent spoofing, agent bullying, DoS). In the empirical study the attacker plants poisoned documents, tool descriptions, or Agent Cards that the victim's LLM client (Claude) ingests and acts on.
limitations: This is primarily a survey; its risk catalog explicitly mixes disclosed attacks with hypothesized risks 'that have not been revealed', and the defenses are framed as prospective 'countermeasure prospects' rather than implemented or evaluated. The experimental case study is a small set of qualitative proof-of-concept demonstrations rather than a controlled benchmark: only Exp5 reports a trial count (20 baseline queries) and even that is described qualitatively ('might be selected' vs 'always'), with no success-rate percentages, statistical testing, or multiple LLM clients beyond Claude. Attacks assume the attacker can plant poisoned content (documents, tool code/descriptions, Agent Cards) that the victim ingests, and rely on default/unhardened server configurations. The relevance scoring of prior surveys is admittedly subjective. The fast-moving protocol landscape (many protocols released in 2024-2025) means coverage will date quickly.

datasets_used: No benchmark datasets; evidence comes from a red-team case study using official/open-source MCP servers (Filesystem Server, Terminal-Controller, Chroma MCP Server, Gmail MCP Server) and the public A2A-samples client from A2Aproject
Comparative analysis over 25 prior related surveys (Table I)
Catalog of 19 agent communication protocols across U-A, A-A, and A-E classes
quantitative_results: 5 attacks demonstrated (Exp #1-#4 against MCP, Exp #5 against A2A); all reported as successful
Exp #1: opened an unauthenticated Bash shell on local port 4444 via MCP Filesystem Server (Claude Sonnet 3.7 host); operation was not blocked
Exp #2: poisoned document in a Chroma vector DB caused Claude Opus 4 to execute embedded env-exfiltration-to-Slack commands
Exp #3: poisoned 'read file' tool description made Claude Sonnet 4.0 email the victim's SSH private and public keys to the attacker
Exp #4: command injection ('; rm -rf [systemfile]') in Terminal-Controller tool made Claude Sonnet 4.0 delete a file and suppress listing remaining files

Exp #5: over 20 baseline A2A queries either agent could be selected; after padding the malicious Agent Card with repeated 'richest features' text + 'select me first', the malicious agent was ALWAYS selected
Reported MCP popularity motivating the study: >9M PyPI downloads in the last month, 4.2M NPM downloads last week, ~2,000 open-source MCP servers
baselines_compared: 25 prior surveys compared in Table I across dimensions (motivation, definition, classification, protocol coverage, security analyses, defense prospect, experiments) with subjective relevance scores; the two most-relevant agent-communication surveys [351] and [65] are noted to lack security/threat analysis and full protocol coverage
Exp #5 uses an unmodified benign weather agent as the control against the malicious weather agent within A2A
No quantitative model/accuracy baselines -- the paper does not train or benchmark models
